acoustic_nodes.description: Snoqualmie Falls 268-foot basalt drop. Broadband noise: 10 Hz to several kHz. Turbulent breakup during fall + chaotic pool impact = full-spectrum broadband. At flood stage: flow volume 2-8x normal, total acoustic energy increases dramatically, sill submerged under massive sheet flow. Gorge geometry (100-foot width at base) creates multi-directional reflection and spatial immersion.

Boulder transport: 30-100+ cm boulders rolling and colliding in flood current, low-frequency impacts (20-200 Hz) felt as much as heard. Rhythm of turbulence: irregular, non-periodic, hydraulic-geometry-scaled not biologically-scaled. Woody debris impacts: large logs and branches hitting basalt canyon walls, brief high-amplitude crack + splash events. The geological percussion rhythm: not human rhythm, not biological rhythm, turbulence rhythm.

Canyon geometry as wind funnel. Falls-driven airflow: falling water mass drags air downward, creates downdraft at base flowing outward along gorge. Thermal channeling: gorge depth creates temperature differential (cool gorge floor vs warm rim), drives consistent gorge wind. Acoustic profile: sustained low-frequency sub-bass roar (20-80 Hz resonant frequencies of gorge cavity). Independent of regional wind conditions.

theory_nodes.concept: Geological timescale principle: three independent simultaneous layers (falls roar, geological percussion, gorge wind) operating on three different driving mechanisms (hydraulic gravity, turbulence, thermal convection) at three different timescales, without synchronization or resolution to common rhythmic structure. The event is not responsive to biological cues -- it continues at geological scale regardless of what biological activity surrounds it. Geological impersonality in acoustic form.
theory_nodes.description: Geological timescale principle -- three independent mechanisms, no synchronization, impersonal scale.

theory_nodes.concept: Snoqualmie Falls 268 feet: basalt sill, Columbia River Basalt formation, King County, 25 miles east Seattle. Watershed 1,700 sq miles Cascade Range. Normal flow: major regional acoustic landmark, audible 0.5 miles. Flood stage (spring snowmelt, 2-8x normal): sill submerged, massive sheet flow over basalt, acoustic output dramatically increased, physiologically overwhelming at close range (exceeds pain threshold at base of gorge plunge pool).
theory_nodes.description: Snoqualmie Falls site -- 268 feet basalt, watershed scale, flood stage acoustic character.

theory_nodes.concept: Boulder transport rhythm: the acoustic 'rhythm' of geological percussion is organized at the scale of hydraulic geometry (turbulence structures, flow velocity gradients, bedform dynamics) not at the scale of biological rhythm. Human rhythmic perception expects period and pulse. Geological percussion has neither. The rhythm is the rhythm of turbulence -- irregular, energetic, organized by physics not biology. This is the acoustic event that We Are the Romans (S36 352) parallels: both operate at the geological rather than human-pulse timescale.
theory_nodes.description: Turbulence rhythm vs biological rhythm -- the key distinction for geological timescale calibration.

theory_nodes.concept: Gorge resonance: canyon geometry creates resonance cavity for low frequencies. Snoqualmie gorge dimensions (100-foot width, 268-foot depth at falls) produce resonant frequencies in the 20-80 Hz range. The gorge amplifies these sub-bass frequencies -- the wind through the gorge at these frequencies is physically felt as chest and body vibration at the gorge bottom. The gorge is a resonance instrument tuned by geological processes.
theory_nodes.description: Gorge resonance cavity: 20-80 Hz resonant frequencies, sub-bass physically felt as body vibration.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Geological timescale pairing. We Are the Romans: three independent compositional voices at simultaneous maximum, not resolving to human-pulse rhythm. Snoqualmie flood: three independent geological layers at simultaneous maximum, not resolving to human-pulse rhythm. Both move at geological scale.

Both riverine flood-stage acoustic events. 339 = flood surge moving downstream (traveling wave, acoustic of flood arrival). 352 = falls at flood stage (stationary site, geological-scale sustained acoustic maximum). Different aspects of the same flood hydrology.

The storm creates the snowpack; the snowmelt creates the flood that brings Snoqualmie Falls to its acoustic maximum. The causality chain: storm (346) → snowpack accumulation → spring melt → flood (352).

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Geological timescale. Both: three independent simultaneous forces at maximum, not resolving to common rhythmic ground, operating at scales larger than human-pulse. We Are the Romans = Bass + Guitar1 + Guitar2 (non-coincident, independent). Snoqualmie flood = Falls + Percussion + Wind (independent, non-resolving). The album moves like the river.
